evidence: In 4 out of 9 sessions, ruinor issued REVISE verdicts that required bitsmith to rework code, followed by re-review. Session 75147a10: ruinor issued REVISE twice before ACCEPT (pathfinder->ruinor REVISE->bitsmith->ruinor REVISE->bitsmith->ruinor ACCEPT). Session 5dc47889: ruinor ACCEPT-WITH-RESERVATIONS on plan, then REVISE on implementation, then ACCEPT after bitsmith fix. Session bd8247d8: ruinor REVISE on bitsmith output, bitsmith reworked, but no follow-up ruinor review is recorded. Session 5f4174d9: ruinor REVISE->pathfinder revise->ruinor ACCEPT->riskmancer REVISE->pathfinder revise->ruinor ACCEPT-WITH-RESERVATIONS. The REVISE->fix->re-review cycle occurs in nearly half of all sessions.
inference: The handoff from ruinor back to bitsmith (or pathfinder) after a REVISE verdict may lack sufficient structured context about exactly what needs to change. Ruinor's output format includes detailed findings with IDs, severity, location, and recommendations, but the orchestrator may not be forwarding the complete structured findings to bitsmith. Alternatively, bitsmith's first implementation attempt may consistently miss quality criteria that ruinor checks, suggesting bitsmith's forge protocol does not include a pre-submission self-check against common ruinor rejection reasons.

proposed_change.change_description: Add a pre-completion self-check to Phase 7 (Final Verification) of the Forge Protocol: 'Before declaring completion, verify against common review criteria: (1) All error paths have explicit handling, (2) Edge cases for boundary conditions are addressed, (3) No missing input validation on public interfaces, (4) Changes are consistent with the plan acceptance criteria. If any of these are not met, address them before signaling completion.'

expected_benefit: Bitsmith's first-pass acceptance rate by ruinor should increase, reducing the number of REVISE->fix->re-review cycles. Expected reduction in multi-round review loops from ~44% of sessions to under 20%.
tradeoffs: Adds cognitive overhead to bitsmith's completion phase. May cause bitsmith to over-check on trivial tasks where these criteria are not relevant. Could slightly increase bitsmith execution time per task.
evaluation_plan: After applying the self-check addition to bitsmith's protocol, observe the next 10 sessions that include ruinor review of bitsmith output. Track: (1) Percentage of sessions where ruinor issues REVISE on first review of bitsmith output. (2) Number of review rounds before ACCEPT. Success: first-pass ACCEPT or ACCEPT-WITH-RESERVATIONS rate increases from ~56% to >75%. Observe for at least 10 sessions before drawing conclusions.

evidence: Sessions 5f4174d9, f008acaf, and 5dc47889 each contain ACCEPT-WITH-RESERVATIONS verdicts from ruinor (5f4174d9 has two such verdicts). Per ruinor's verdict definitions, ACCEPT-WITH-RESERVATIONS means 'Acceptable to proceed, but noted issues should be addressed during implementation' or 'noted issues should be addressed.' However, no mechanism exists in the chronicle data or orchestrator config to track these reservations, ensure they are addressed in subsequent work, or carry them forward as technical debt items.
inference: The ACCEPT-WITH-RESERVATIONS verdict is effectively treated as ACCEPT by the orchestrator. The reservations are voiced by ruinor but lost because there is no persistence mechanism to track them. This means known issues flagged by the reviewer are systematically dropped, accumulating as invisible technical debt.

proposed_change.change_description: Add to Phase 5 (Completion): 'When any reviewer issues ACCEPT-WITH-RESERVATIONS, extract the reservations from the review findings and append them to plans/open-questions.md under a section titled "Review Reservations - [session date]" with the specific issues noted. These become tracked items for future sessions.'

expected_benefit: Reservations from reviews are persisted and trackable. Technical debt items flagged by reviewers are not silently dropped. Future planning sessions can reference open reservations when scoping related work.
tradeoffs: Adds a write step to the orchestrator's completion phase. open-questions.md could grow large if reservations accumulate without being addressed. May create noise if reservations are consistently minor/cosmetic.
evaluation_plan: After applying the change, observe the next 5 sessions that produce ACCEPT-WITH-RESERVATIONS verdicts. Success: reservations appear in plans/open-questions.md with specific issues documented. Check after 10 sessions whether any documented reservations have been referenced or addressed in subsequent planning. Failure: orchestrator still treats ACCEPT-WITH-RESERVATIONS as ACCEPT without persisting reservations.

evidence: Originally observed in sessions 2134216b (5 consecutive bitsmith calls) and 7eb84ce4 (3 consecutive bitsmith calls). After the fix was applied to the orchestrator config on 2026-03-23, the pattern continues in new sessions: a185aa8b shows 9 bitsmith invocations after ruinor ACCEPT at 01:26:00Z (bitsmith calls at 01:26:47Z, 01:27:16Z, 01:34:45Z, 01:37:42Z, 01:49:21Z, 01:50:51Z, 01:52:48Z, 01:56:51Z, 01:58:31Z) with no intermediate ruinor review. Session db946bcb shows 5 consecutive bitsmith invocations after ruinor ACCEPT at 04:29:37Z (bitsmith at 04:30:04Z, 04:36:33Z, 04:38:00Z, 04:40:55Z, 04:48:58Z, 04:51:23Z, 05:00:06Z) with no intermediate review. Fix was applied but problem persists across 4 confirmed sessions.
inference: The wording addition to Phase 3 of the orchestrator config ('run Ruinor review after each logically complete unit of work') is being interpreted permissively. The orchestrator treats the entire post-approval implementation batch as a single 'logically complete unit' rather than subdividing it. The constraint needs to be more prescriptive: naming a concrete maximum number of bitsmith invocations before a mandatory intermediate review checkpoint.

proposed_change.change_description: Strengthen the Phase 3 intermediate review constraint: replace the 'logically complete unit' language with a concrete rule — 'After every 2 consecutive bitsmith invocations without an intervening ruinor review, run an intermediate ruinor review before continuing. Do not accumulate more than 2 unreviewed bitsmith completions in sequence.' This removes the ambiguity of 'logically complete unit' by providing a numerical threshold.

expected_benefit: Eliminates chains of 5–9 unreviewed bitsmith invocations. Catches defects at the 2-call mark rather than at session end, reducing rework scope.
tradeoffs: May produce unnecessary review calls when multiple bitsmith tasks are truly independent and small (e.g., updating 3 separate config files). The threshold of 2 is somewhat arbitrary; 3 might be a better balance between review coverage and latency.
evaluation_plan: After applying the numerical threshold, observe the next 10 sessions with multiple bitsmith invocations. Success: no chain of more than 2 consecutive bitsmith completions without an intervening ruinor review. Failure: chains of 3+ still appear, indicating the constraint is not being honored. Observe for at least 10 sessions.

evidence: In 5 sessions, bitsmith is invoked after quill completes with no subsequent ruinor review gate: session db946bcb (quill at 04:30:56Z, followed by bitsmith at 04:36:33Z, 04:38:00Z, 04:40:55Z, 04:48:58Z, 04:51:23Z, 05:00:06Z — 6 unreviewed bitsmith calls); session 8b0e8d3b (quill at 03:21:43Z, followed by 5 bitsmith calls through 03:41:37Z, no ruinor); session ecd4168d (quill at 03:52:38Z, followed by bitsmith at 04:25:11Z and 04:25:42Z, no ruinor); session 27936ba3 (quill at 03:13:10Z, followed by bitsmith at 03:16:37Z, no ruinor); session c960cd13 (quill at 14:30:22Z, followed by riskmancer and then bitsmith at 14:38:28Z, no ruinor). In all five cases, implementation work continues after the documentation phase without re-invoking the mandatory review gate.
inference: Quill's invocation in Phase 5b of the completion workflow is being treated as a session-terminating event rather than an intermediate step. When post-documentation implementation work is needed (e.g., applying minor fixes discovered during documentation review, or implementing quill-directed changes), the orchestrator does not route this work back through Phase 4's ruinor review gate. The completion workflow's 5b/5c structure implies documentation comes after implementation is finalized — but in practice, quill is being invoked while implementation work is still outstanding, and subsequent bitsmith invocations are treated as continuation of an already-reviewed implementation rather than new unreviewed work.

proposed_change.change_description: Add a constraint to Phase 5b (Documentation update): 'Quill must only be invoked after Phase 4 implementation review is fully complete and all reviewers have issued ACCEPT or ACCEPT-WITH-RESERVATIONS. If any bitsmith implementation work is needed after Quill completes, that work must re-enter Phase 4 (Implementation Review) before the session can be declared complete — do not treat post-documentation bitsmith invocations as pre-reviewed work.'

expected_benefit: Post-quill bitsmith work is subject to ruinor review, closing the gap where documentation-phase implementation changes ship unreviewed. Eliminates 5+ session pattern where quill marks an informal end-of-review boundary.
tradeoffs: If post-quill bitsmith work is genuinely trivial (e.g., fixing a markdown link quill flagged), requiring a full ruinor review may add unnecessary latency. A tiered threshold (ruinor for code changes, skip for docs-only changes) would be more precise but adds complexity to the rule.
evaluation_plan: After applying the constraint, observe the next 10 sessions where quill is invoked. Success: zero sessions with bitsmith invocations occurring after quill without an intervening ruinor review. Failure: post-quill unreviewed bitsmith invocations still appear, indicating the pattern is driven by a structural gap not addressed by this constraint. Observe for at least 10 sessions.
